subject: Maths
read_aloud: What do you add to 7 to make 10?
correct_response: Yes! 7 plus 3 makes 10. You nailed it, Reuben!
incorrect_response: Not quite! Try counting up from 7 on your fingers: 8, 9, 10. That's 3 jumps! So 7 plus 3 equals 10.
hint: Try counting up from 7 until you reach 10.
mnemonic: 7 and 3 are best friends that always make 10!

subject: Maths
read_aloud: What do you add to 4 to make 10?
correct_response: Brilliant! 4 plus 6 equals 10. You know your number bonds so well!
incorrect_response: Nearly! Think of 10 as two hands. If you hold up 4 fingers, how many are still down? That's right, 6 fingers are down. So 4 plus 6 makes 10.
hint: Hold up 4 fingers. How many fingers are still down?
mnemonic: 4 and 6 go together like two halves of your 10 fingers.
metacognitive_prompt: How did you figure that out?

subject: Maths
read_aloud: What do you add to 9 to make 10?
correct_response: Spot on! 9 is so close to 10, you just need 1 more!
incorrect_response: Not quite! 9 is really close to 10. You only need 1 more to get there. So 9 plus 1 equals 10.
hint: 9 is almost 10. How many more do you need?

subject: Maths
read_aloud: What do you add to 2 to make 10?
correct_response: Amazing! 2 plus 8 makes 10. You're flying through these!
incorrect_response: Think about a ten-frame with 2 squares filled in. How many empty squares are left? That's 8! So 2 plus 8 makes 10.
hint: Imagine a ten-frame. Fill in 2. How many empty spaces?

subject: Maths
correct_response: That's true! 5 plus 5 is 10. Double 5 makes 10! Two high-fives!
incorrect_response: Actually, it is true! 5 plus 5 does equal 10. Think of your two hands: each hand has 5 fingers, and together they make 10.
hint: Think about both your hands. Each one has 5 fingers.

subject: Maths
correct_response: Fantastic! 8 plus 5 is 13. You're a superstar!
incorrect_response: Good try! Here's a trick called bridging through 10: take 2 from the 5 to make the 8 into 10. Now you have 10 plus 3, which is 13!
hint: Can you make 10 first? What do you add to 8 to make 10, and how much is left over?

subject: Maths
correct_response: Yes! 7 plus 6 is 13. Brilliant mental maths!
incorrect_response: Nearly there! Try using near doubles: 7 plus 7 is 14, and 6 is one less than 7, so 7 plus 6 is one less than 14, which is 13!
hint: 7 plus 7 is 14. Since 6 is one less than 7, what's one less than 14?

subject: Maths
correct_response: Incredible! 9 plus 7 is 16. You're on fire today!
incorrect_response: Good effort! Try bridging through 10: take 1 from the 7 to make the 9 into 10. Now you have 10 plus 6, which is 16.
hint: Make 10 first! What do you add to 9 to get 10? Then add what's left of the 7.

subject: Maths
correct_response: Awesome! 6 plus 8 equals 14. You smashed it!
incorrect_response: Not quite! Here's a neat trick: 6 plus 8 is the same as double 7. 6 is one less than 7, and 8 is one more than 7. So 6 plus 8 equals 14, just like 7 plus 7!
hint: Try near doubles. 7 plus 7 is 14. 6 is one less and 8 is one more than 7.

subject: Maths
read_aloud: What is 5 plus 9? Is it A, 13, B, 14, or C, 15?
correct_response: Yes, it's B! 5 plus 9 is 14. Great work!
incorrect_response: Close! Try bridging through 10: take 1 from the 5 to turn the 9 into 10. Now it's 10 plus 4, which gives you 14. The answer is B!
hint: Turn the 9 into 10 by borrowing 1 from the 5. What's left?

subject: Maths
correct_response: Wonderful! 15 minus 8 is 7. You've got a great brain for this!
incorrect_response: Good try! Here's how to think about it: subtract 5 from 15 first to get 10, then subtract the remaining 3 to get 7. Breaking it into steps makes it easier!
hint: Try subtracting to 10 first. 15 minus 5 is 10, then take away 3 more.

subject: Maths
correct_response: That's right! 13 take away 6 is 7. Superb!
incorrect_response: Not quite, but you're learning! Try this: take away 3 from 13 to get to 10, then take away the other 3 to get 7. Bridging through 10 is a really powerful trick!
hint: Go down to 10 first. 13 minus 3 is 10. How much more do you still need to take away?
metacognitive_prompt: How did you work that out?

subject: Maths
correct_response: Brilliant! 17 minus 9 is 8. You make it look easy!
incorrect_response: Nearly! Here's a clever shortcut called compensating: 9 is nearly 10, so do 17 minus 10 which is 7, then add 1 back because you took away one too many. That gives you 8!
hint: 9 is close to 10. Try taking away 10 first, then adjusting.
mnemonic: When you subtract 9, subtract 10 and add 1 back!

subject: Maths
correct_response: Yes! 14 minus 7 is 7. That's a halving fact too, since double 7 is 14!
incorrect_response: Good effort! Here's a clue: if you know that 7 plus 7 is 14, then 14 minus 7 must be 7. Doubles facts help with subtraction too!
hint: Do you know what double 7 is? That can help you here.

subject: Maths
correct_response: Spot on! 16 minus 7 equals 9. Fantastic work!
incorrect_response: Almost! Try going down to 10 first: 16 minus 6 gets you to 10, and then you need to take 1 more away to make it minus 7 altogether. 10 minus 1 is 9!
hint: Bridge through 10. Take away 6 to get to 10, then take away 1 more.

subject: Maths
correct_response: Amazing! 34 plus 25 is 59. You handled those big numbers like a pro!
incorrect_response: Good try! Use partitioning: split the numbers into tens and ones. 30 plus 20 is 50, and 4 plus 5 is 9. Put them together and you get 59!
hint: Break the numbers apart. Add the tens first, then add the ones.

subject: Maths
correct_response: Brilliant! 46 plus 23 equals 69. Top marks!
incorrect_response: Nearly! Try partitioning: break 46 into 40 and 6, and 23 into 20 and 3. Now add the tens: 40 plus 20 is 60. Then add the ones: 6 plus 3 is 9. Put them together to get 69!
hint: Split each number into tens and ones, then add each part.
metacognitive_prompt: What strategy did you use to add those?

subject: Maths
correct_response: Wow! 27 plus 38 is 65. That one had carrying and you still got it!
incorrect_response: Nice try! This one is a bit tricky because the ones add up past 10. Try partitioning: 20 plus 30 is 50, and 7 plus 8 is 15. Now add 50 and 15 together to get 65!
hint: Add the tens first: 20 plus 30. Then add the ones: 7 plus 8. Combine them.

subject: Maths
read_aloud: What is 53 plus 29? Is it A, 72, B, 82, or C, 81?
correct_response: It's B, 82! Great job, Reuben!
incorrect_response: Close! Try compensating: 29 is nearly 30, so do 53 plus 30 which is 83, then subtract 1 because you added one too many. That gives you 82, which is B!
hint: 29 is close to 30. Try adding 30 first, then adjust.

subject: Maths
correct_response: Superb! 45 plus 36 is 81. You're getting really good at two-digit addition!
incorrect_response: Good effort! Let's partition: 40 plus 30 is 70, and 5 plus 6 is 11. Now add 70 and 11 together to get 81. When the ones go over 10, just carry on adding!
hint: Split into tens and ones. Don't forget: if the ones add up to more than 10, keep going.

subject: Maths
correct_response: Yes! 2 times 6 is 12. You know your 2 times table well!
incorrect_response: Not quite! 2 times 6 means two groups of 6, or you can think of it as double 6. Double 6 is 12!
hint: 2 times a number means double it. What is double 6?

subject: Maths
correct_response: Fantastic! 2 times 8 is 16. Double 8 done in a flash!
incorrect_response: Good try! 2 times 8 is the same as double 8. Think of 8 plus 8, which is 16. You can use your number bonds: 8 plus 8 makes 16!
hint: Double 8. What is 8 plus 8?

subject: Maths
correct_response: Brilliant! 2 times 9 is 18. You're a times table champion!
incorrect_response: Nearly! 2 times 9 means double 9. Think of it as 9 plus 9. You can bridge through 10: 9 plus 1 is 10, plus 8 more is 18!
hint: What is 9 plus 9? That's the same as 2 times 9.
metacognitive_prompt: How did you figure that out?

subject: Maths
correct_response: Spot on! 2 times 7 is 14. Double 7 is a piece of cake for you!
incorrect_response: Close! 2 times 7 is the same as 7 plus 7. Double 7 is 14!
hint: What is 7 plus 7?

subject: Maths
correct_response: That's false! Well spotted! 2 times 12 is actually 24, not 22. You didn't fall for the trick!
incorrect_response: Actually, this one is false! 2 times 12 means double 12. Double 10 is 20, and double 2 is 4, so double 12 is 24, not 22. Partitioning works for doubling too!
hint: Double 12. Try splitting 12 into 10 and 2, and doubling each part.

subject: Maths
correct_response: Yes! 5 times 4 is 20. The 5 times table is so satisfying!
incorrect_response: Not quite! Count in fives four times: 5, 10, 15, 20. So 5 times 4 is 20. Every answer in the 5 times table ends in 0 or 5!
hint: Count in 5s: 5, 10, 15... What comes next?
mnemonic: 5 times table answers always end in 0 or 5!

subject: Maths
correct_response: Amazing! 5 times 7 is 35. You counted in fives like a superstar!
incorrect_response: Good try! Count in fives seven times: 5, 10, 15, 20, 25, 30, 35. So 5 times 7 is 35. Remember, when you times an odd number by 5, the answer ends in 5!
hint: Count in 5s seven times, or think of 5 times 7 as half of 10 times 7.

subject: Maths
correct_response: Fantastic! 5 times 9 is 45. You're flying through these!
incorrect_response: Nearly! Here's a cool trick: 5 times a number is the same as half of 10 times that number. 10 times 9 is 90, and half of 90 is 45!
hint: What is 10 times 9? Now halve it!
mnemonic: 5 times is always half of 10 times!

subject: Maths
read_aloud: What is 5 times 6? Is it A, 25, B, 30, or C, 35?
correct_response: It's B! 5 times 6 is 30. Brilliant!
incorrect_response: Not quite! Count in fives six times: 5, 10, 15, 20, 25, 30. The answer is 30, which is B. When you times an even number by 5, the answer always ends in 0!
hint: 5 times an even number always ends in 0. Count in 5s six times.

subject: Maths
correct_response: Wow! 5 times 11 is 55. You went beyond the basic table and nailed it!
incorrect_response: Good effort! Try this: you know 5 times 10 is 50. Since 11 is one more than 10, just add one more 5. That gives you 55!
hint: Start with 5 times 10, which is 50. Then add one more 5.

subject: Maths
correct_response: Yes! 10 times 3 is 30. The 10 times table is your secret weapon!
incorrect_response: Not quite! The 10 times table has a brilliant pattern: just put a 0 on the end of the number. 3 with a 0 on the end is 30!
hint: For the 10 times table, just stick a zero on the end!

subject: Maths
correct_response: Spot on! 10 times 7 is 70. The 10 times table is the easiest one!
incorrect_response: Almost! Remember the pattern: 10 times any number just means putting a zero after it. So 10 times 7 is 70!
hint: What happens when you put a zero after the 7?

subject: Maths
correct_response: Incredible! 10 times 12 is 120. You handled a three-digit answer like a champ!
incorrect_response: Good try! Even with bigger numbers, the rule stays the same: 10 times 12 means put a zero after 12, which gives you 120!
hint: The 10 times rule works with any number. Just add a zero after 12.

subject: Maths
correct_response: That's false! Good catch! 10 times 5 is 50, not 55. 55 would be 5 times 11!
incorrect_response: This one is false! 10 times 5 is 50, not 55. Remember, 10 times a number puts a zero at the end, so it's 50. Be careful not to mix up the 10 times table and the 5 times table!
hint: Use the 10 times table rule. What do you get when you put a zero after 5?

subject: Maths
correct_response: Fabulous! 10 times 9 is 90. Quick as a flash!
incorrect_response: Not quite! Remember, with the 10 times table you just pop a zero on the end. 9 becomes 90! Every answer in the 10 times table ends in zero.
hint: Add a zero to the end of 9.
metacognitive_prompt: How did you figure that out?

subject: Maths
correct_response: Superb! 72 minus 38 is 34. That's a tough one and you cracked it!
incorrect_response: Good effort on a tricky question! Try compensating: 38 is close to 40. So do 72 minus 40 which is 32, then add 2 back because you took away 2 too many. That gives you 34!
hint: 38 is close to 40. Try subtracting 40 first, then adjust.

subject: Maths
correct_response: Amazing! 63 minus 27 is 36. You're becoming a subtraction expert!
incorrect_response: Nearly! Let's use partitioning: 63 minus 20 is 43, then 43 minus 7 is 36. Take away the tens first, then the ones. It makes big subtractions much easier!
hint: Subtract in two steps: take away the 20 first, then take away the 7.

subject: Maths
correct_response: Brilliant! 85 minus 49 is 36. That compensating trick is working well for you!
incorrect_response: Good try! Since 49 is very close to 50, try compensating: 85 minus 50 is 35, but you subtracted 1 too many, so add 1 back to get 36!
hint: 49 is almost 50. Subtract 50 and then adjust by adding 1 back.

subject: Maths
read_aloud: What is 54 minus 28? Is it A, 24, B, 26, or C, 32?
correct_response: It's B, 26! Wonderful work, Reuben!
incorrect_response: Close! Try compensating: 28 is near 30. 54 minus 30 is 24, then add 2 back because you subtracted 2 too many. That gives you 26, which is B!
hint: 28 is close to 30. Subtract 30 from 54 and then add 2 back.

subject: Maths
correct_response: Superb! 91 minus 45 is 46. You handled that tricky subtraction beautifully!
incorrect_response: Good effort! Try partitioning: 91 minus 40 is 51, then 51 minus 5 is 46. Breaking it into smaller steps makes it much more manageable!
hint: Take away the tens first: 91 minus 40. Then subtract the remaining 5.

subject: Maths
read_aloud: Share 12 sweets between 3 children. How many sweets does each child get?
correct_response: Yes! 12 shared between 3 is 4 each. Fair shares for everyone!
incorrect_response: Not quite! Imagine dealing out the sweets one by one to each of the 3 children: 1 each, that's 3 gone. 2 each, that's 6 gone. 3 each, that's 9 gone. 4 each, that's all 12 gone! So each child gets 4.
hint: Imagine giving one sweet to each child at a time. Keep going until all 12 are shared out.

subject: Maths
read_aloud: Share 20 stickers between 5 friends. How many stickers does each friend get?
correct_response: Brilliant! 20 divided by 5 is 4. Each friend gets 4 stickers!
incorrect_response: Good try! Think about your 5 times table: what times 5 makes 20? 4 times 5 is 20! So each friend gets 4 stickers. Your times tables help with division!
hint: Think about your 5 times table. What number times 5 equals 20?
mnemonic: Division and times tables are best friends. If you know one, you know the other!
metacognitive_prompt: How did you figure that out?

subject: Maths
read_aloud: There are 18 socks. You put them in pairs. How many pairs do you make?
correct_response: Spot on! 18 divided by 2 is 9 pairs. That's a lot of matching socks!
incorrect_response: Nearly! Putting into pairs means dividing by 2. Think about your 2 times table: 2 times 9 is 18, so 18 divided by 2 is 9 pairs!
hint: Pairs means groups of 2. How many 2s make 18?

subject: Maths
read_aloud: Share 30 grapes between 10 bowls. How many grapes in each bowl?
correct_response: Fantastic! 30 divided by 10 is 3. Each bowl gets 3 grapes!
incorrect_response: Good effort! Think about your 10 times table: what times 10 makes 30? 3 times 10 is 30, so each bowl gets 3 grapes!
hint: Use your 10 times table. What number times 10 gives you 30?

subject: Maths
read_aloud: You have 15 marbles and you put them into groups of 5. How many groups do you make? Is it A, 2, B, 3, or C, 4?
correct_response: It's B! 15 divided by 5 is 3 groups. Well done!
incorrect_response: Not quite! Count in 5s up to 15: 5, 10, 15. That took 3 jumps! So 15 divided by 5 is 3 groups. The answer is B!
hint: Count in 5s: 5, 10, 15. How many jumps did that take?

subject: Maths
read_aloud: Reuben has 23 football cards. His friend gives him 19 more. How many football cards does he have now?
correct_response: Yes! 23 plus 19 is 42 football cards! What a collection!
incorrect_response: Good try! The word 'more' tells us to add. Try compensating: 19 is close to 20, so do 23 plus 20 which is 43, then take away 1 because you added 1 too many. That gives you 42!
hint: You need to add because he gets more cards. Try adding 20 first, then adjusting.
metacognitive_prompt: What clue in the question told you to add?

subject: Maths
read_aloud: There are 35 children on a bus. 18 children get off at the school stop. How many children are still on the bus?
correct_response: Spot on! 35 minus 18 is 17. You read that problem perfectly!
incorrect_response: Almost! When children get off, we need to subtract. Try compensating: 18 is close to 20. 35 minus 20 is 15, then add 2 back because you subtracted 2 too many. That gives you 17!
hint: Children getting off means you need to subtract. 18 is close to 20, try that first.

subject: Maths
read_aloud: A baker puts 5 biscuits on each tray. She fills 6 trays. How many biscuits has she baked altogether?
correct_response: Amazing! 5 times 6 is 30 biscuits. That's a lot of baking!
incorrect_response: Good try! The key word is 'each', which tells us every tray has the same amount. That means we multiply: 5 times 6. Count in 5s six times: 5, 10, 15, 20, 25, 30!
hint: If each tray has 5 biscuits and there are 6 trays, can you use your 5 times table?
metacognitive_prompt: What strategy did you use to solve that?

subject: Maths
read_aloud: Reuben saves 10p every day for a week. How much money has he saved altogether?
correct_response: Brilliant! 10 times 7 is 70. He saved 70p in a week!
incorrect_response: Nearly! A week has 7 days. If Reuben saves 10p each day, that's 10 times 7. Use your 10 times table: 10 times 7 is 70p!
hint: How many days are in a week? Now use your 10 times table.

subject: Maths
read_aloud: There are 24 children in the class. The teacher puts them into teams of 2. How many teams are there? Is it A, 10, B, 12, or C, 14?
correct_response: It's B, 12 teams! 24 divided by 2 is 12. Fantastic problem-solving!
incorrect_response: Close! Groups of 2 means we divide by 2, which is the same as halving. Half of 24 is 12, so there are 12 teams. The answer is B! Halving is a really useful skill for division by 2.
hint: Dividing by 2 is the same as halving. What is half of 24?
metacognitive_prompt: How did you work that out?
